content.question: What are the differences between var, let, and const in JavaScript? When should you use each?
content.answer:
var, let, and const are the three ways to declare variables in JavaScript, each with different scoping rules and behaviors.

var is function-scoped, can be redeclared and reassigned, and is hoisted with an initial value of undefined. It was the only option before ES6 and is generally avoided in modern code due to its loose scoping.

let is block-scoped, cannot be redeclared in the same scope, can be reassigned, and lives in the Temporal Dead Zone until its declaration. Use let when the variable's value will change.

const is block-scoped like let, but cannot be reassigned after initialization. However, const objects and arrays can still have their contents mutated. Use const by default for all variables whose reference won't change.

codeExamples.output:
var - function scoped
b is not defined
c is not defined
codeExamples.explanation: var leaks out of blocks into the enclosing function. let and const are confined to the block where they are declared.

codeExamples.title: Redeclaration and Reassignment
codeExamples.explanation: var allows both redeclaration and reassignment. let allows only reassignment. const prevents reassignment but not property mutation.

codeExamples.title: Loop Behavior
codeExamples.explanation: var creates one shared variable for all iterations. let creates a fresh binding for each iteration, which closures capture independently.

notes: Default to const, use let only when reassignment is needed, avoid var entirely
const does not make values immutable - use Object.freeze() for that
In a for loop, let creates a new binding per iteration which solves closure issues
followUps: How would you make a const object truly immutable?
What happens if you declare a var inside a block but not inside a function?
Can you use const in a for loop?
interviewTips: Always mention the scoping difference first - it is the most important distinction
Demonstrate the loop closure problem as a practical example
State your preference: const by default, let when needed, never var

content.question: What are the different data types in JavaScript? How do you check types reliably?
content.answer:
JavaScript has 7 primitive types and 1 reference type. Primitives are immutable and stored by value: string, number, bigint, boolean, undefined, null, and symbol. Everything else (objects, arrays, functions, dates, regex) is a reference type stored by reference.

The typeof operator is the primary way to check types, but it has quirks. typeof null returns 'object' (a historical bug), and typeof for any function returns 'function'. For arrays and other specific objects, use Array.isArray() or instanceof.

For robust type checking, combining typeof, instanceof, and Object.prototype.toString.call() covers all edge cases. Understanding the difference between primitive values and their wrapper objects (String vs string) is also crucial.

codeExamples.title: Primitive Types and typeof
codeExamples.code:
console.log(typeof 'hello');     // string
console.log(typeof 42);          // number
console.log(typeof 42n);         // bigint
console.log(typeof true);        // boolean
console.log(typeof undefined);   // undefined
console.log(typeof Symbol('x')); // symbol
console.log(typeof null);        // object (bug!)
console.log(typeof {});          // object
console.log(typeof []);          // object
console.log(typeof function(){}); // function
codeExamples.output:
string
number
bigint
boolean
undefined
symbol
object
object
object
function
codeExamples.explanation: typeof works for most primitives but fails for null (returns 'object') and cannot distinguish arrays from objects.

codeExamples.title: Reliable Type Checking
codeExamples.code:
// Array check
console.log(Array.isArray([1, 2]));    // true
console.log(Array.isArray({length: 2})); // false

// instanceof
console.log([] instanceof Array);   // true
console.log({} instanceof Object);  // true
console.log(new Date() instanceof Date); // true

// Object.prototype.toString - most reliable
const typeOf = (val) => Object.prototype.toString.call(val).slice(8, -1);
console.log(typeOf(null));      // Null
console.log(typeOf([]));        // Array
console.log(typeOf({}));        // Object
console.log(typeOf(new Date())); // Date
console.log(typeOf(/regex/));   // RegExp

true
Null

codeExamples.explanation: Object.prototype.toString.call() is the most reliable way to check any value's type, returning the internal [[Class]] tag.

codeExamples.title: Value vs Reference
codeExamples.explanation: Primitives compare by value. Objects compare by reference - two identical-looking objects are not equal unless they reference the same memory.

typeof null === 'object' is a well-known bug that will never be fixed for backwards compatibility
NaN is of type number but NaN !== NaN; use Number.isNaN() to check

interviewTips: Memorize all 7 primitives - interviewers love asking for the complete list
Always mention the typeof null bug proactively to show depth of knowledge
Explain value vs reference with a mutation example to make it concrete

content.question: Explain short-circuit evaluation in JavaScript. How do &&, ||, and ?? differ?
content.answer:
Short-circuit evaluation means JavaScript stops evaluating a logical expression as soon as the result is determined. With &&, if the left side is falsy, it returns immediately without evaluating the right side. With ||, if the left side is truthy, it returns immediately.

The || operator returns the first truthy value or the last value. It treats all falsy values the same: 0, '', null, undefined, NaN, false. The ?? (nullish coalescing) operator was introduced to solve this - it only short-circuits on null or undefined, not on other falsy values like 0 or ''.

Optional chaining (?.) is related - it short-circuits property access when the left side is null or undefined, returning undefined instead of throwing a TypeError.

codeExamples.title: Short-Circuit with && and ||
codeExamples.code:
// || returns first truthy value (or last value)
console.log(null || 'default');    // 'default'
console.log('hello' || 'default'); // 'hello'
console.log(0 || 42);              // 42
console.log('' || 'fallback');     // 'fallback'

// && returns first falsy value (or last value)
console.log('hello' && 'world');   // 'world'
console.log(null && 'world');      // null
console.log(1 && 2 && 3);          // 3
console.log(1 && 0 && 3);          // 0
codeExamples.explanation: || finds the first truthy operand, && finds the first falsy operand. Neither returns true/false - they return the actual operand value.

codeExamples.title: Nullish Coalescing (??) vs OR (||)
codeExamples.explanation: ?? only treats null and undefined as 'missing'. || treats all falsy values (0, '', false, NaN) as 'missing'. Use ?? when 0 or '' are valid values.

codeExamples.title: Optional Chaining (?.)
codeExamples.output:
Mumbai
undefined
undefined
Not provided
1
undefined
codeExamples.explanation: Optional chaining returns undefined instead of throwing when accessing properties on null/undefined. It pairs perfectly with ?? for default values.

notes: ?? has lower precedence than || and && - use parentheses when mixing them
Optional chaining short-circuits the entire chain: a?.b.c returns undefined if a is null
Short-circuit && is commonly used for conditional rendering in React: {isLoggedIn && <Dashboard />}
followUps: Can you mix ?? with || or && without parentheses?
What is the difference between user?.name and user && user.name?
How does short-circuit evaluation help with performance?
interviewTips: Show a real-world example where || fails and ?? succeeds (port = 0 is the classic case)
Mention that && and || return operand values, not booleans
Connect optional chaining to real API response handling where nested properties may not exist

content.question: What are the most important string methods in JavaScript? Explain template literals and tagged templates.
content.answer:
Strings in JavaScript are immutable - every string method returns a new string rather than modifying the original. Key methods include slice(), substring(), includes(), startsWith(), endsWith(), indexOf(), replace(), replaceAll(), split(), trim(), padStart(), padEnd(), and repeat().

Template literals (backtick strings) allow embedded expressions with ${}, multi-line strings without escape characters, and tagged template functions. Tagged templates let you parse template literals with a custom function, which is useful for sanitization, internationalization, and DSLs like styled-components.

Modern JavaScript also provides at() for negative indexing and matchAll() for regex iteration.

codeExamples.title: Essential String Methods
codeExamples.code:
const str = '  Hello, JavaScript World!  ';

// Searching
console.log(str.includes('JavaScript'));  // true
console.log(str.indexOf('World'));        // 19
console.log(str.startsWith('  Hello'));   // true

// Extracting
console.log(str.trim());                 // 'Hello, JavaScript World!'
console.log(str.trim().slice(7, 17));    // 'JavaScript'
console.log(str.trim().split(', '));     // ['Hello', 'JavaScript World!']

// Transforming
console.log('hello'.toUpperCase());      // 'HELLO'
console.log('abc'.repeat(3));            // 'abcabcabc'
console.log('5'.padStart(3, '0'));       // '005'
console.log('hello'.replace('l', 'L')); // 'heLlo' (first only)
console.log('hello'.replaceAll('l', 'L')); // 'heLLo'

19

JavaScript
['Hello', 'JavaScript World!']
HELLO
abcabcabc
005

codeExamples.explanation: All methods return new strings. replace() only replaces the first match; use replaceAll() or a regex with /g for all matches.

interviewTips: Know the difference between slice and substring - slice supports negative indices
Mention tagged templates to show advanced knowledge beyond basic interpolation

content.question: Explain the key array methods in JavaScript. How do map, filter, and reduce differ?
content.answer:
JavaScript arrays have powerful built-in methods that enable functional-style programming. The three most important are map, filter, and reduce.

map() transforms each element and returns a new array of the same length. filter() returns a new array containing only elements that pass a test function. reduce() accumulates all elements into a single value using an accumulator.

Other essential methods include find() (first match), findIndex() (index of first match), some() (any match?), every() (all match?), flat() (flatten nested arrays), flatMap() (map + flatten), and sort() (in-place sorting). Understanding method chaining - combining these methods - is critical for writing clean, readable code.

codeExamples.title: map, filter, and reduce
codeExamples.explanation: map returns a new array of same length. filter returns a subset. reduce collapses everything into one value. None mutate the original array.

codeExamples.title: Method Chaining
codeExamples.explanation: Chaining filter -> map -> reduce is the most common pattern. Reduce is versatile - it can group, flatten, count, or build any data structure.

codeExamples.title: find, some, every, flat
codeExamples.explanation: find returns the element (not a boolean). some/every return booleans. flat accepts a depth argument. flatMap is equivalent to map().flat(1).

notes: map, filter, reduce, find, flat, flatMap all return new arrays (non-mutating)
sort() and reverse() mutate the original array - use toSorted() and toReversed() for non-mutating versions
reduce can implement any other array method - it is the most powerful but also the hardest to read
followUps: How would you implement map using reduce?
What is the difference between find and filter?
How does sort() work with numbers vs strings?
interviewTips: Be comfortable writing reduce from scratch - it is the most asked array method
Show chaining fluency: filter().map().reduce() in a single expression
Know which methods mutate (push, pop, sort, splice) vs which create new arrays (map, filter, slice)

content.question: Explain destructuring assignment and the spread/rest operator in JavaScript. How are they used in practice?
content.answer:
Destructuring assignment lets you unpack values from arrays or properties from objects into distinct variables. It provides a clean, readable syntax for extracting data and is used extensively in modern JavaScript and React.

The spread operator (...) expands an iterable (array, object, string) into individual elements. It is used for copying arrays/objects, merging, and passing array elements as function arguments.

The rest parameter (...) collects remaining elements into an array or remaining properties into an object. Despite using the same ... syntax as spread, rest is the opposite operation - it gathers instead of expanding. The distinction depends on context: spread appears in array literals and function calls, rest appears in function parameters and destructuring patterns.

codeExamples.title: Object and Array Destructuring
codeExamples.explanation: Destructuring supports defaults, renaming, skipping, nesting, and variable swapping. It mirrors the structure of the data.

codeExamples.title: Spread Operator
codeExamples.explanation: Spread creates shallow copies. Later properties override earlier ones during object merge. Nested objects share references.

codeExamples.title: Rest Parameters
codeExamples.explanation: Rest collects remaining items. In objects, it is perfect for removing sensitive fields. In React, it is used for props forwarding.

notes: Spread creates a shallow copy - nested objects still share references
Rest must be the last element in destructuring or parameter lists
Destructuring works in function parameters: function({ name, age }) {}
followUps: How would you deep clone an object without spread?
What is the difference between arguments and rest parameters?
Can you use destructuring in a for...of loop?
interviewTips: Show the password-removal pattern with rest - interviewers love practical examples
Always mention that spread is a shallow copy to demonstrate awareness of the pitfall
Know that rest replaces the old arguments object, which is array-like but not a real array

content.question: How does error handling work in JavaScript? Explain try/catch/finally and custom error classes.
content.answer:
JavaScript uses try/catch/finally blocks for synchronous error handling. The try block contains code that might throw. The catch block handles the error. The finally block runs regardless of whether an error occurred - it is guaranteed to execute even if catch throws or try returns.

JavaScript has several built-in error types: Error, TypeError, ReferenceError, SyntaxError, RangeError, and URIError. Each has a name, message, and stack trace.

Custom error classes extend the built-in Error class to create domain-specific errors. This is essential in production code for distinguishing between different error types (validation errors, authentication errors, not-found errors) and handling each appropriately.

For asynchronous code, try/catch works with async/await. For promises without await, use .catch(). Unhandled rejections should be caught with a global handler.

Cleanup: always runs

Cleanup: always runs

from try
codeExamples.explanation: finally ALWAYS executes - after return, after throw, after catch. It is perfect for cleanup like closing connections or releasing resources.

Custom error classes show production-level thinking about error architecture

content.question: What is the difference between == and === in JavaScript? When should you use each?
content.answer:
The == (loose equality) operator compares values after performing type coercion, meaning it converts operands to the same type before comparing. The === (strict equality) operator compares both value and type without any coercion.

Best practice: Always use === (strict equality) to avoid unexpected bugs from type coercion. Use == only when you intentionally want type coercion (e.g., checking for null or undefined with value == null).

codeExamples.title: Loose vs Strict Equality
codeExamples.code:
// Loose equality (==) - performs type coercion
console.log(5 == "5");      // true (string coerced to number)
console.log(0 == false);     // true (false coerced to 0)
console.log("" == false);    // true (both coerced to 0)
console.log(null == undefined); // true (special rule)

// Strict equality (===) - no coercion
console.log(5 === "5");      // false (different types)
console.log(0 === false);     // false (number vs boolean)
console.log("" === false);   // false (string vs boolean)
console.log(null === undefined); // false (different types)
codeExamples.output:
true
true
true
true
false
false
false
false
codeExamples.explanation: With ==, JavaScript tries to convert both sides to the same type. With ===, types must match.

codeExamples.title: Common Pitfalls
codeExamples.code:
// Surprising coercion results
console.log([] == false);    // true
console.log([] == ![]);      // true (both coerce to 0)
console.log("0" == false);   // true
console.log("" == 0);        // true
console.log(NaN == NaN);      // false (NaN is never equal to anything)
console.log(NaN === NaN);     // false

// Safe null/undefined check
let val = null;
console.log(val == null);       // true
console.log(val == undefined);  // true
console.log(val === null);      // true
console.log(val === undefined); // false
codeExamples.output:
true
true
true
true
false
false
true
true
true
false
codeExamples.explanation: The == null check is a common pattern to test for both null and undefined in one expression.

notes: NaN is not equal to anything, including itself - use Number.isNaN() to check
Objects are compared by reference, not by value, with both == and ===
null == undefined is true, but null === undefined is false
followUps: How does Object.is() differ from ===?

When is using == actually preferred over ===?
interviewTips: Always recommend === as the default and explain why
Mention the null == undefined shorthand as a valid == use case
Knowing the NaN quirk shows depth of understanding

content.question: What are arrow functions in JavaScript? How do they differ from regular functions?
content.answer:
Arrow functions (=>) are a concise syntax for writing functions introduced in ES6. They differ from regular functions in several key ways:

1. No own "this" - arrow functions inherit "this" from the enclosing lexical scope
2. No "arguments" object - use rest parameters (...args) instead
3. Cannot be used as constructors - cannot use "new" keyword
4. No prototype property
5. Cannot be used as generator functions
6. Implicit return for single expressions

codeExamples.title: Syntax Variations
codeExamples.explanation: Arrow functions can have implicit returns and various parameter styles.

codeExamples.title: "this" Behavior Difference
codeExamples.explanation: The regular function callback gets its own "this" (global/undefined). The arrow function inherits "this" from startArrow, which is obj.

notes: Arrow functions are ideal for callbacks and array methods like map, filter, reduce
Do NOT use arrow functions as object methods - they won't have the correct "this"
Arrow functions cannot be used with call(), apply(), or bind() to change "this"
followUps: Why can't arrow functions be used as constructors?
When should you NOT use arrow functions?
How do arrow functions handle the arguments object?
interviewTips: The "this" difference is the most important distinction - always lead with it
Give a practical example like setTimeout or array methods
Mention when NOT to use arrow functions (methods, prototypes)

content.question: What are template literals in JavaScript? What features do they provide over regular strings?
content.answer:
Template literals are string literals enclosed in backticks (`) that provide:

1. String interpolation - embed expressions with ${expression}
2. Multiline strings - no need for \n or string concatenation
3. Tagged templates - process template literals with a function
4. Raw strings - access raw string content without escape processing

codeExamples.explanation: Template literals make string building much cleaner than concatenation.

interviewTips: Show practical examples like building URLs or HTML strings

content.question: What is the difference between null and undefined in JavaScript?
content.answer:
Both null and undefined represent "absence of value" but they serve different purposes:

- undefined: variable has been declared but not assigned a value. It is the default value assigned by JavaScript.
- null: an intentional assignment indicating "no value" or "empty". It is explicitly set by the programmer.

Key differences:
1. typeof undefined === "undefined", typeof null === "object" (historic bug)
2. undefined is the default for uninitialized variables, missing function args, missing object properties
3. null must be explicitly assigned
4. null == undefined is true (loose), null === undefined is false (strict)

codeExamples.title: undefined Scenarios
codeExamples.explanation: JavaScript assigns undefined automatically in these cases.

codeExamples.title: null Usage
codeExamples.explanation: null is used intentionally. The == null check is a common shorthand for checking both null and undefined.

notes: typeof null === "object" is a known bug from the first JavaScript implementation
Use val == null to check for both null and undefined at once
undefined is a type itself, null is an object (technically)
JSON.stringify ignores undefined properties but keeps null
followUps: Why does typeof null return "object"?
How does nullish coalescing (??) handle null vs undefined?
What does void 0 return and why was it used?
interviewTips: Emphasize the semantic difference: undefined = unset, null = intentionally empty
The typeof null === "object" bug is a classic JS trivia question
Show the val == null shorthand pattern - it demonstrates practical knowledge

content.question: What is JSON? Explain JSON.parse() and JSON.stringify() with their common pitfalls.
content.answer:
JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a lightweight data interchange format. Despite the name, it is language-independent and used widely for API communication and configuration.

- JSON.stringify(): Converts a JavaScript object into a JSON string
- JSON.parse(): Converts a JSON string back into a JavaScript object

Common pitfalls:
1. undefined, functions, and Symbols are omitted during stringify
2. NaN and Infinity become null
3. Date objects become strings (not converted back by parse)
4. Circular references throw an error
5. JSON.parse of invalid JSON throws SyntaxError

codeExamples.title: Basic Usage
codeExamples.explanation: JSON.stringify converts to string, JSON.parse converts back. The third argument of stringify controls indentation.

codeExamples.title: Pitfalls & Edge Cases
codeExamples.explanation: Functions, undefined, and Symbols are silently removed. NaN/Infinity become null. The parse+stringify trick creates a deep clone but loses special types.

notes: Always wrap JSON.parse() in a try-catch for untrusted input
Use the replacer parameter of stringify to filter or transform values
For deep cloning, prefer structuredClone() over JSON.parse(JSON.stringify())
JSON keys must be double-quoted strings - single quotes are invalid
followUps: What is the replacer and reviver parameter in JSON methods?
How would you handle circular references in JSON?
What is the toJSON() method?
interviewTips: Always mention the types that are lost during serialization
The deep clone trick is commonly asked - mention structuredClone as the modern alternative
Knowing about replacer/reviver shows advanced understanding

content.question: What is a closure in JavaScript? Explain with examples and real-world use cases.
content.answer:
A closure is a function that remembers and has access to variables from its outer (enclosing) function's scope, even after the outer function has returned. Closures are created every time a function is created.

Key points:
1. A closure gives you access to an outer function's scope from an inner function.
2. Closures are commonly used for data privacy, function factories, and partial application.
3. Variables in a closure are references (not copies), so they reflect the latest value.

codeExamples.explanation: inner() forms a closure over 'count'. Even after outer() returns, inner() still has access to count.

codeExamples.title: Data Privacy with Closures
codeExamples.explanation: balance is private - it can only be accessed through the returned methods. This is the module pattern.

content.question: Explain hoisting in JavaScript. How do var, let, const, and function declarations behave differently?
content.answer:
Hoisting is JavaScript's default behavior of moving declarations to the top of their scope during the compilation phase. However, only declarations are hoisted, not initializations.

- var: hoisted and initialized with undefined
- let/const: hoisted but NOT initialized (Temporal Dead Zone)
- Function declarations: fully hoisted (both name and body)
- Function expressions: only the variable is hoisted (not the function body)

codeExamples.title: var Hoisting
codeExamples.explanation: var declarations are hoisted and initialized with undefined. The assignment stays in place.

codeExamples.title: let/const and Temporal Dead Zone
codeExamples.explanation: let and const are hoisted but remain in a 'Temporal Dead Zone' until the declaration line is reached.

codeExamples.title: Function Declaration vs Expression
codeExamples.output:
Hello, Alice!
farewell is not a function
codeExamples.explanation: Function declarations are fully hoisted. Function expressions with var hoist the variable as undefined, causing a TypeError when called.

notes: Hoisting happens during the creation phase of the execution context
let and const ARE hoisted - they just can't be accessed before declaration (TDZ)
Class declarations are also in the TDZ like let/const
followUps: What exactly happens during the creation phase?
Why was the TDZ introduced with let/const?
What is the output of: console.log(typeof undeclaredVar)?
interviewTips: Many candidates say let/const are not hoisted - this is wrong. They ARE hoisted but in TDZ
Always explain the WHY - TDZ prevents using variables before they're ready
Draw a timeline showing declaration phase vs execution phase

content.question: What are the key Object static methods? Explain property descriptors and immutability in JavaScript objects.
content.answer:
JavaScript provides several static methods on the Object constructor for working with objects. Object.keys() returns an array of own enumerable property names. Object.values() returns the values. Object.entries() returns key-value pairs as arrays. Object.assign() copies properties from source objects to a target.

Property descriptors control how individual properties behave. Each property has attributes: value, writable (can be changed), enumerable (shows in for...in), and configurable (can be deleted or reconfigured). Object.defineProperty() lets you set these attributes.

For immutability, Object.freeze() makes an object completely immutable (shallow), Object.seal() prevents adding/removing properties but allows modification, and Object.preventExtensions() only prevents adding new properties.

codeExamples.title: Object.keys, values, entries
codeExamples.explanation: Object.entries and Object.fromEntries let you transform objects using array methods. Object.hasOwn replaces the older hasOwnProperty method.

codeExamples.title: Property Descriptors
codeExamples.output:
42
42
25
{get: [Function: get], set: [Function: set], enumerable: true, configurable: false}
codeExamples.explanation: Property descriptors give fine-grained control over how properties behave. Getters/setters let you run logic on property access.

codeExamples.title: freeze, seal, preventExtensions
codeExamples.explanation: freeze is shallow - nested objects are still mutable. You need a recursive deepFreeze to make the entire object tree immutable.

notes: Object.freeze is shallow - nested objects remain mutable
Object.hasOwn() is the modern replacement for obj.hasOwnProperty()
Property descriptors default to false for all flags when using Object.defineProperty
followUps: How would you implement a deep freeze function?
What is the difference between Object.assign and the spread operator?
When would you use Object.create(null)?
interviewTips: Always mention that Object.freeze is shallow - this catches many candidates off guard
Show that you know Object.entries + Object.fromEntries for object transformation
Distinguish freeze vs seal vs preventExtensions clearly with a comparison table

content.question: Explain how 'this' works in JavaScript. What are the binding rules and how do arrow functions differ?
content.answer:
The 'this' keyword in JavaScript is determined by how a function is called, not where it is defined. There are four binding rules in order of precedence:

1. new binding: When a function is called with 'new', this refers to the newly created object.
2. Explicit binding: call(), apply(), or bind() explicitly set this.
3. Implicit binding: When a function is called as a method of an object, this refers to that object.
4. Default binding: In non-strict mode, this defaults to the global object (window/global). In strict mode, this is undefined.

Arrow functions are a special case - they do not have their own 'this'. Instead, they inherit 'this' from the enclosing lexical scope at the time they are defined. This makes them ideal for callbacks and event handlers where you want to preserve the outer 'this'.

codeExamples.title: The Four Binding Rules
codeExamples.output:
undefined
Hello, Alice
Hello, Bob
Charlie
codeExamples.explanation: Binding precedence: new > explicit (call/apply/bind) > implicit (method call) > default (standalone call).

codeExamples.title: Common 'this' Pitfall
codeExamples.output:
undefined knows Bob
undefined knows Charlie
---
Alice knows Bob
Alice knows Charlie
codeExamples.explanation: Regular functions in callbacks lose the outer 'this'. Arrow functions solve this by inheriting from the enclosing scope.

codeExamples.title: Arrow Functions and this
codeExamples.explanation: Arrow functions have no own 'this', no 'arguments', no 'prototype', and cannot be used with 'new'. Their 'this' is permanently set at creation time.

notes: Arrow functions do not have their own this, arguments, super, or new.target
In event handlers, 'this' refers to the element that triggered the event (unless using arrow functions)
bind() returns a new function with 'this' permanently set - it cannot be overridden
followUps: What does 'this' refer to in a class method vs a regular function?
How does 'this' work in event listeners?
Can you explain why setTimeout callbacks lose 'this'?
interviewTips: Draw a decision tree: new > call/apply/bind > obj.method > standalone
The forEach callback losing 'this' is the most common practical example
Always mention arrow functions as the modern solution to 'this' confusion

content.question: What are call, apply, and bind in JavaScript? How do they differ and when would you use each?
content.answer:
call, apply, and bind are methods on Function.prototype that let you explicitly set 'this' for a function invocation.

call() invokes the function immediately with a given 'this' and arguments passed individually: fn.call(thisArg, arg1, arg2).

apply() is identical to call but takes arguments as an array: fn.apply(thisArg, [arg1, arg2]). A mnemonic: Apply takes an Array.

bind() does NOT invoke the function. Instead, it returns a new function with 'this' permanently bound. This is useful when you need to pass a function reference that should maintain a specific 'this' context, like event handlers or callbacks.

Common use cases include function borrowing (using a method from one object on another), partial application (pre-filling some arguments), and preserving context in callbacks.

codeExamples.title: call vs apply vs bind
codeExamples.output:
Hello, I'm Alice!
Hi, I'm Bob.
Hey, I'm Alice!!
Hey, I'm Alice?
codeExamples.explanation: call and apply invoke immediately. bind returns a new function. bind also supports partial application - pre-filling the first argument.

codeExamples.title: Function Borrowing
codeExamples.explanation: Function borrowing lets you use methods from one object on another. Array.prototype.slice.call(arguments) was the classic way to convert array-like objects.

codeExamples.title: bind in Event Handlers and Classes
codeExamples.output:
Submit clicked 1 time(s)
Submit clicked 2 time(s)
10
15
codeExamples.explanation: bind in constructors ensures methods keep their 'this' when used as callbacks. Partial application with bind pre-fills arguments to create specialized functions.

notes: call and apply invoke immediately; bind returns a new function
A bound function's this cannot be overridden - not even with call or apply
Modern alternative to apply: spread operator (Math.max(...arr) vs Math.max.apply(null, arr))
followUps: Can you implement your own bind function?
What happens if you call bind on an already-bound function?
How does bind work with arrow functions?
interviewTips: Mnemonic: Call = Comma-separated args, Apply = Array of args
Show the practical class constructor pattern - binding methods in the constructor
Implementing a polyfill for bind is a very common coding interview question

content.question: Explain prototypal inheritance in JavaScript. What is the prototype chain?
content.answer:
JavaScript uses prototypal inheritance, where objects can inherit properties and methods from other objects. Every object has an internal [[Prototype]] link (accessible via __proto__ or Object.getPrototypeOf()) that points to another object.

When you access a property on an object, JavaScript first looks at the object itself. If not found, it follows the prototype chain - checking the object's prototype, then the prototype's prototype, and so on until it reaches null (the end of the chain).

Functions have a 'prototype' property (not to be confused with __proto__). When you use 'new' to create an instance, the instance's __proto__ is set to the constructor's prototype. This is how methods are shared across all instances without duplicating them in memory.

Object.create() lets you create objects with a specific prototype, enabling clean prototypal inheritance without constructors.

codeExamples.title: Prototype Chain Basics
codeExamples.output:
Rex says Woof
Animal
true
false
true
true
null
codeExamples.explanation: dog inherits from animal, which inherits from Object.prototype. Property lookup walks up this chain until it finds the property or reaches null.

codeExamples.title: Constructor Functions and prototype
codeExamples.output:
Hi, I'm Alice, age 25
Hi, I'm Bob, age 30
true
true
true
true
codeExamples.explanation: Methods defined on the prototype are shared (one copy in memory). Each instance's __proto__ points to Person.prototype.

codeExamples.title: Prototype-based Inheritance
codeExamples.explanation: Shape.call(this, color) borrows the parent constructor. Object.create(Shape.prototype) sets up the chain so Circle instances can access Shape methods.

notes: __proto__ is the actual link in the prototype chain; .prototype is a property of constructor functions
Object.create(null) creates an object with no prototype - useful for pure dictionaries
Modern code uses class syntax, but it is syntactic sugar over prototypes
followUps: What is the difference between __proto__ and prototype?
How does instanceof work under the hood?
What are the advantages of prototypal over classical inheritance?
interviewTips: Draw the prototype chain: instance -> Constructor.prototype -> Object.prototype -> null
Explain that class syntax is sugar over prototypes - proving you understand the underlying mechanism
Mention that hasOwnProperty checks only the object itself, not the prototype chain

content.question: Explain ES6 classes in JavaScript. How do inheritance, static methods, and private fields work?
content.answer:
ES6 classes are syntactic sugar over JavaScript's prototype-based inheritance. They provide a clearer, more familiar syntax for creating constructor functions and setting up prototype chains.

A class body can contain a constructor, instance methods, static methods, getters/setters, and private fields (prefixed with #). The 'extends' keyword creates a subclass, and 'super' calls the parent class constructor or methods.

Static methods belong to the class itself, not instances. Private fields (ES2022) with the # prefix are truly private - they cannot be accessed outside the class, unlike the older _convention which was just a naming hint.

Despite the class syntax, understanding that classes are functions and inheritance is prototypal remains important for interviews.

codeExamples.title: Class Basics with Inheritance
codeExamples.output:
Rex makes a sound
Buddy makes a sound (actually: Buddy barks)
true
true
codeExamples.explanation: extends sets up the prototype chain. super() in the constructor calls the parent constructor. super.method() calls the parent's version of an overridden method.

codeExamples.title: Private Fields and Getters/Setters
codeExamples.explanation: Private fields with # are truly private - accessing them outside the class is a SyntaxError. Getters/setters let you control property access with method logic.

codeExamples.title: Static Methods and Properties
codeExamples.explanation: Static methods and properties belong to the class, not instances. They are useful for utility functions and factory methods. Private static methods use #.

notes: Classes are 'syntactic sugar' over prototypes - typeof MyClass === 'function'
Private fields (#) are a true language feature, not just a convention like _underscore
super() must be called before accessing 'this' in a derived class constructor
followUps: Can you have multiple inheritance in JavaScript?
What are mixins and how do they work with classes?
What is the difference between class fields and constructor assignments?
interviewTips: Emphasize that classes are syntactic sugar - show typeof MyClass === 'function'
Demonstrate private fields over the _convention to show you know modern JavaScript
Be ready to explain why super() must come before 'this' in derived constructors

content.question: Explain Promises in depth. How do Promise.all, Promise.race, Promise.allSettled, and Promise.any differ?
content.answer:
A Promise represents an asynchronous operation that will eventually resolve with a value or reject with a reason. Promises have three states: pending, fulfilled, and rejected. Once settled (fulfilled or rejected), a promise's state cannot change.

The .then() method handles fulfilled values, .catch() handles rejections, and .finally() runs regardless of outcome. Promises chain naturally - each .then() returns a new promise.

Promise combinators handle multiple promises:
- Promise.all(): Resolves when ALL promises fulfill; rejects if ANY one rejects.
- Promise.allSettled(): Waits for ALL to settle; never rejects. Returns status and value/reason for each.
- Promise.race(): Settles as soon as the FIRST promise settles (fulfill or reject).
- Promise.any(): Resolves with the FIRST fulfilled promise; rejects only if ALL reject (AggregateError).

codeExamples.title: Promise Basics and Chaining
codeExamples.explanation: Returning a promise from .then() chains them sequentially. Errors propagate down the chain to the nearest .catch(). finally runs regardless.

codeExamples.title: Promise.all vs Promise.allSettled
codeExamples.output:
all: ['fast', 'slow']
all error: error
OK: fast
FAIL: error
OK: slow
codeExamples.explanation: Promise.all short-circuits on first rejection. Promise.allSettled always waits for all and returns {status, value/reason} for each.

codeExamples.title: Promise.race vs Promise.any
codeExamples.output:
race error: p3 failed
any won: p2
any all failed: ['err1', 'err2']
codeExamples.explanation: race settles with whatever finishes first (even rejections). any only cares about fulfillments and rejects only when all promises reject.

notes: Promise constructor callback runs synchronously - only .then/.catch are async
Unhandled promise rejections can crash Node.js - always add .catch() or use try/catch with await
Promise.resolve(value) and Promise.reject(reason) create pre-settled promises
followUps: How would you implement a timeout wrapper for a promise?
What is the difference between throwing in .then() and calling reject()?
How do you run promises in sequence (not parallel)?
interviewTips: Know all four combinators and when to use each - this is frequently asked
Show a practical example: Promise.all for parallel API calls, Promise.race for timeouts
Emphasize that Promise constructor runs synchronously - this trips up many candidates

content.question: Explain async/await in JavaScript. What are the common patterns and pitfalls?
content.answer:
async/await is syntactic sugar over Promises that makes asynchronous code look synchronous. An async function always returns a Promise. The await keyword pauses execution until the promise settles, then returns its resolved value or throws the rejection.

Common patterns include sequential execution (await one after another), parallel execution (Promise.all with await), error handling (try/catch blocks), and retry logic. A major pitfall is accidentally running async operations sequentially when they could run in parallel.

Key rules: await can only be used inside async functions (or at the top level of ES modules). Error handling with try/catch replaces .then/.catch chains. If you forget to await a promise, you get the promise object instead of the value.

codeExamples.title: Sequential vs Parallel Execution
codeExamples.output:
Parallel: A, B in ~100ms
Sequential: A, B in ~200ms
codeExamples.explanation: Sequential awaits finish one after another (200ms total). Promise.all runs them in parallel (100ms total). Always use parallel when operations are independent.

codeExamples.title: Error Handling Patterns
codeExamples.explanation: Three error handling patterns: classic try/catch, .catch() for inline fallbacks, and Go-style error tuples for clean handling without try/catch blocks.

codeExamples.title: Async Iteration and Retry Pattern
codeExamples.output:
['HELLO', 'WORLD']
Attempt 1 failed: not yet
Attempt 2 failed: not yet
Retry result: success
codeExamples.explanation: for...of with await processes items sequentially. The retry pattern is essential for network requests. Note: forEach does NOT await - use for...of instead.

notes: async functions ALWAYS return a promise, even if you return a plain value
await only pauses the async function, not the entire program
forEach with async callbacks does not await - use for...of or Promise.all(arr.map())
followUps: What is the difference between return and return await inside async functions?
How does top-level await work in ES modules?
How would you limit concurrency when processing many async operations?
interviewTips: Always show the sequential vs parallel comparison - it demonstrates practical awareness
Mention the forEach pitfall - this is a common source of bugs
Know when to use for...of (sequential) vs Promise.all(map()) (parallel)

content.question: Explain type coercion in JavaScript. What are the rules for == vs === and truthy/falsy values?
content.answer:
Type coercion is JavaScript's automatic conversion of values from one type to another. It happens in two contexts: implicit coercion (triggered by operators and comparisons) and explicit coercion (using functions like Number(), String(), Boolean()).

The == operator performs type coercion before comparison, while === (strict equality) does not - it requires both value AND type to match. The == rules are complex: null == undefined is true, numbers are compared numerically, and objects are converted with valueOf/toString.

There are exactly 8 falsy values in JavaScript: false, 0, -0, 0n (BigInt zero), '' (empty string), null, undefined, and NaN. Everything else is truthy, including empty objects {}, empty arrays [], and the string 'false'.

Understanding coercion is essential because it causes many subtle bugs, especially with ==, + (concatenation vs addition), and if conditions.

codeExamples.title: == vs === and Coercion Rules
codeExamples.code:
// Strict equality - no coercion
console.log(1 === '1');    // false (different types)
console.log(1 === 1);     // true
console.log(null === undefined); // false

// Loose equality - with coercion
console.log(1 == '1');    // true (string -> number)
console.log(true == 1);   // true (boolean -> number)
console.log(null == undefined); // true (special rule)
console.log(null == 0);   // false (null only == undefined)
console.log('' == false);  // true (both -> 0)

// Surprising results
console.log([] == false);  // true ([] -> '' -> 0, false -> 0)
console.log([] == ![]);    // true ([] -> 0, ![] -> false -> 0)
console.log('' == 0);     // true
console.log(' \t\n' == 0); // true (whitespace string -> 0)
codeExamples.output:
false
true
false
true
true
true
false
true
true
true
true
true
codeExamples.explanation: == coerces types before comparing. The rules are: null == undefined, string/boolean convert to number, objects convert via valueOf/toString. Always use === to avoid surprises.

codeExamples.title: Truthy/Falsy Values
codeExamples.output:
false      -> false
0          -> false
0          -> false
0          -> false
           -> false
null       -> false
undefined  -> false
NaN        -> false
---
0              -> true
false          -> true
               -> true
[object Object] -> true
function(){} -> true
port defined
codeExamples.explanation: Only 8 values are falsy. Empty arrays, empty objects, and the string '0' are all truthy. Use explicit checks when 0 or '' are valid values.

codeExamples.title: The + Operator Coercion
codeExamples.code:
// + with strings = concatenation
console.log('5' + 3);       // '53' (number -> string)
console.log(5 + '3');       // '53' (number -> string)
console.log('5' + true);    // '5true'

// + with numbers = addition
console.log(5 + 3);          // 8
console.log(true + true);    // 2 (boolean -> number)
console.log(null + 1);       // 1 (null -> 0)

// Other operators always convert to number
console.log('6' - 1);       // 5 (string -> number)
console.log('6' * '2');      // 12
console.log('abc' - 1);     // NaN

// Explicit conversion
console.log(Number(''));     // 0
console.log(Number('abc'));  // NaN
console.log(Number(true));   // 1
console.log(Number(null));   // 0
console.log(Number(undefined)); // NaN
codeExamples.explanation: The + operator prefers string concatenation if either operand is a string. Other math operators (-, *, /) always convert to numbers.

notes: Always use === unless you have a specific reason for == (like null == undefined check)
The + operator is the most confusing - it does concatenation with strings, addition with numbers
Number(undefined) is NaN but Number(null) is 0 - a common gotcha
followUps: What is the output of {} + [] and [] + {}?
How does the Symbol.toPrimitive method work?
Why is NaN !== NaN?
interviewTips: Memorize the 8 falsy values - interviewers love asking for the complete list
State clearly: always use === in production code
The + coercion rules are the most commonly tested - know string vs number behavior

content.question: Compare CommonJS and ES Modules. What are the key differences and when do you use each?
content.answer:
JavaScript has two module systems: CommonJS (CJS) used primarily in Node.js, and ES Modules (ESM) which is the standard for both browsers and modern Node.js.

CommonJS uses require() for importing and module.exports for exporting. It is synchronous, evaluated at runtime, and supports dynamic require paths. It was designed for server-side use.

ES Modules use import/export syntax. They are asynchronous, statically analyzed at parse time (enabling tree-shaking), and have strict mode enabled by default. ESM supports named exports, default exports, and dynamic import() for code splitting.

Key differences: ESM is statically analyzable (imports must be at the top level), CJS is fully dynamic. ESM exports are live bindings (reflect changes), CJS exports are copied values. ESM uses 'import.meta' for metadata, CJS uses '__filename' and '__dirname'.

codeExamples.title: CommonJS vs ES Modules Syntax
codeExamples.explanation: CJS uses require/module.exports, ESM uses import/export. ESM allows both named and default exports. CJS destructuring happens at runtime, ESM bindings are resolved at parse time.

codeExamples.title: Dynamic Imports and Code Splitting
codeExamples.explanation: Dynamic import() is the standard way to do code splitting. It returns a Promise with the module namespace. React.lazy uses this for component-level splitting.

codeExamples.title: Live Bindings vs Value Copies
codeExamples.explanation: CJS copies the value at require time. ESM creates a live reference that always reflects the current value. However, ESM imports are read-only from the consumer side.

notes: Use ESM for new projects - it is the standard and enables tree-shaking
In Node.js, use .mjs extension or set "type": "module" in package.json for ESM
Dynamic import() works everywhere and is the basis for code splitting
followUps: What is tree-shaking and why does it require ESM?
How do you use CommonJS modules from an ESM file?
What is the 'dual package hazard' in Node.js?
interviewTips: Clearly state the key difference: ESM is static (parse-time), CJS is dynamic (runtime)
Mention tree-shaking as the killer feature of ESM for bundlers
The live bindings vs copied values distinction is a common follow-up question

content.question: How do you efficiently manipulate the DOM in JavaScript? Explain key DOM methods and event handling.
content.answer:
The Document Object Model (DOM) is a tree representation of an HTML document. JavaScript interacts with it through the DOM API to read, modify, create, and delete elements.

Key selection methods: getElementById, querySelector (CSS selector), querySelectorAll (returns NodeList), getElementsByClassName and getElementsByTagName (return live HTMLCollections).

For manipulation: createElement, appendChild, insertBefore, removeChild, replaceChild, and the modern append, prepend, before, after, replaceWith, remove methods. innerHTML and textContent set content (innerHTML parses HTML, textContent is safer and faster).

Performance tips: Batch DOM operations using DocumentFragment or building strings before innerHTML. Minimize layout thrashing by reading all measurements before writing. Use event delegation instead of attaching listeners to many elements.

codeExamples.title: DOM Selection and Manipulation
codeExamples.output:
• New Item
  • Second Item
    • 2
codeExamples.explanation: querySelector uses CSS selectors. createElement + textContent + classList is the safe way to build elements. Modern methods (append, prepend, before, after) are cleaner than older APIs.

codeExamples.title: Efficient Batch DOM Updates
codeExamples.output:
DocumentFragment approach: 1 reflow
innerHTML approach: 1 reflow
Individual appendChild: N reflows
codeExamples.explanation: DocumentFragment is a lightweight container that is not part of the DOM tree. Appending it triggers a single reflow. innerHTML is fast but be careful with user input (XSS risk).

codeExamples.title: Event Handling
codeExamples.output: Event listener options: capture, once, passive, signal
codeExamples.explanation: addEventListener supports options: once (auto-remove), capture (capture phase), passive (no preventDefault for scroll perf), and signal (AbortController cleanup).

notes: querySelector returns the first match; querySelectorAll returns a static NodeList
Use textContent over innerHTML to prevent XSS attacks with user-generated content
AbortController is the modern way to clean up multiple event listeners at once
followUps: What is the difference between HTMLCollection and NodeList?
How does virtual DOM (React) compare to direct DOM manipulation?
What causes layout thrashing and how do you prevent it?
interviewTips: Mention DocumentFragment for batch operations - it shows performance awareness
Know the security difference: innerHTML allows XSS, textContent does not
The AbortController pattern for cleanup is modern and impressive to interviewers

content.question: Explain event bubbling, capturing, and delegation. Why is event delegation important?
content.answer:
When an event fires on a DOM element, it goes through three phases:
1. Capturing phase: The event travels from the window down to the target element.
2. Target phase: The event reaches the target element.
3. Bubbling phase: The event bubbles back up from the target to the window.

By default, event listeners fire during the bubbling phase. You can listen during the capture phase by passing { capture: true } to addEventListener.

Event delegation is a pattern where you attach a single event listener to a parent element instead of individual listeners to each child. When a child fires an event, it bubbles up to the parent where you check event.target to determine which child was clicked.

Benefits: Better memory usage (fewer listeners), automatically handles dynamically added elements, and simpler code. This is the same principle React uses under the hood with its synthetic event system.

codeExamples.title: Event Bubbling and Capturing
codeExamples.output: Click order: Outer(cap) -> Inner(cap) -> Button -> Inner(bub) -> Outer(bub)
codeExamples.explanation: Events travel down (capture) then back up (bubble). Most code uses bubbling. Capture is rare but useful for intercepting events before they reach targets.

codeExamples.title: Event Delegation Pattern
codeExamples.output: New item added - click handler works without new listener!
codeExamples.explanation: One listener on the parent handles all children. event.target identifies the clicked element. target.closest() finds the ancestor. Dynamically added elements are handled automatically.

codeExamples.title: stopPropagation and preventDefault
codeExamples.output:
stopPropagation: prevents bubbling
preventDefault: prevents default browser action
stopImmediatePropagation: prevents same-element handlers
codeExamples.explanation: stopPropagation stops the event from reaching other elements. preventDefault stops default behavior (form submit, link navigation). stopImmediatePropagation stops other handlers on the same element.

notes: Event delegation works because of bubbling - events travel from target up to the root
Use event.target for the actual clicked element, event.currentTarget for the listener element
React uses a single event listener at the root (event delegation) for all events
followUps: Which events do NOT bubble? (focus, blur, scroll, mouseenter, mouseleave)
How does React's synthetic event system use delegation?
What is the difference between event.target and event.currentTarget?
interviewTips: Show the todo list example - it is the clearest demonstration of delegation benefits
Mention that delegation handles dynamically added elements automatically
Know which events do not bubble - focus/blur use focusin/focusout for delegation

content.question: Explain the key Web APIs: localStorage, sessionStorage, and fetch. How do you use them effectively?
content.answer:
Web APIs provide browser capabilities accessible through JavaScript. The most commonly used in interviews are Storage APIs and the Fetch API.

localStorage persists data with no expiration. sessionStorage persists only for the browser tab session. Both store key-value pairs as strings (5-10MB limit). Use JSON.stringify/parse for objects.

The Fetch API is the modern way to make HTTP requests, replacing XMLHttpRequest. It returns Promises, supports streaming, AbortController for cancellation, and various request options. Key gotcha: fetch does NOT reject on HTTP errors (404, 500) - you must check response.ok.

Other important Web APIs include: IntersectionObserver (lazy loading), ResizeObserver (element size changes), MutationObserver (DOM changes), requestAnimationFrame (smooth animations), Web Workers (background threads), and the Clipboard API.

codeExamples.title: localStorage and sessionStorage
codeExamples.explanation: localStorage persists across browser sessions. Always wrap in try/catch (private browsing may throw). The expiry wrapper adds TTL functionality that localStorage lacks natively.

codeExamples.title: Fetch API with Error Handling
codeExamples.output: fetch does NOT reject on HTTP errors - always check response.ok
codeExamples.explanation: fetch only rejects on network failures, NOT HTTP errors. Always check response.ok. POST requires explicit Content-Type header and JSON.stringify for the body.

codeExamples.title: AbortController for Request Cancellation
codeExamples.explanation: AbortController cancels fetch requests by passing its signal. Aborted fetches throw an AbortError. This prevents race conditions in search-as-you-type features.

notes: localStorage has a ~5MB limit per origin and stores only strings
fetch does NOT throw on HTTP errors - only on network failures
AbortController works for fetch requests and also for addEventListener cleanup
followUps: What is the difference between localStorage and cookies?
How do you handle CORS issues with fetch?
What is the difference between response.json() and JSON.parse(response.text())?
interviewTips: Always mention that fetch does not reject on 404/500 - this is the most common mistake
Show the AbortController pattern for search cancellation - it demonstrates production awareness
Know localStorage limits: ~5MB, strings only, synchronous, same-origin

content.question: Explain event bubbling and capturing in JavaScript. How does the event flow work in the DOM?
content.answer:
When an event occurs on a DOM element, it goes through three phases:

1. Capturing Phase (top-down): Event travels from window down to the target element
2. Target Phase: Event reaches the target element
3. Bubbling Phase (bottom-up): Event bubbles up from the target back to window

By default, event listeners fire during the bubbling phase. You can listen during the capturing phase by passing { capture: true } as the third argument to addEventListener.

Key methods:
- event.stopPropagation(): Stops event from propagating further
- event.stopImmediatePropagation(): Stops propagation AND prevents other handlers on same element
- event.preventDefault(): Prevents default browser behavior (not related to propagation)

codeExamples.title: Bubbling Demonstration
codeExamples.output:
Button - Bubble
Inner - Bubble
Outer - Bubble
codeExamples.explanation: The event starts at the button (target) and bubbles up through parent elements.

codeExamples.title: Capturing Phase & stopPropagation
codeExamples.explanation: stopPropagation prevents the event from reaching further elements in both capturing and bubbling.

notes: Most events bubble, but focus, blur, mouseenter, and mouseleave do not
Event delegation relies on bubbling - attach one listener to a parent element
event.target is the clicked element; event.currentTarget is where the listener is attached
Use { once: true } to auto-remove a listener after it fires once
followUps: What is event delegation and why is it useful?

What is the difference between target and currentTarget?
