Mention practical use: one listener on a list parent instead of each list item

content.question: What are higher-order functions in JavaScript? Give practical examples.
content.answer:
A higher-order function is a function that does at least one of the following:

1. Takes one or more functions as arguments (callbacks)
2. Returns a function as its result

Higher-order functions are a core concept in functional programming and are used extensively in JavaScript. Built-in examples include Array.prototype.map, filter, reduce, forEach, sort, and setTimeout.

codeExamples.title: Functions as Arguments
codeExamples.explanation: map, filter, reduce all accept a function as an argument. repeat is a custom HOF.

codeExamples.title: Functions Returning Functions
codeExamples.explanation: These are function factories - functions that create and return specialized functions.

notes: Higher-order functions enable composition and code reuse
Array methods like map, filter, reduce are the most common examples
Closures and higher-order functions often work together
They are a key building block of functional programming in JavaScript
followUps: How would you implement your own map function?
What is function composition?
What is the difference between a callback and a higher-order function?
interviewTips: Start with the definition, then show built-in examples (map, filter)
Show a custom higher-order function to demonstrate deeper understanding
Connect it to functional programming concepts like composition and immutability

content.question: What is callback hell? How do Promises and async/await solve it?
content.answer:
Callback hell (also called the "pyramid of doom") occurs when multiple asynchronous operations are nested inside callbacks, making the code deeply indented and hard to read.

Evolution of async patterns:
1. Callbacks: Simple but leads to nesting and error handling issues
2. Promises: Chain .then() calls, centralized error handling with .catch()
3. Async/await: Synchronous-looking code, try/catch for errors

Async/await is syntactic sugar over Promises and is the preferred modern approach.

codeExamples.title: Callback Hell vs Promise Chain
codeExamples.explanation: Promises flatten the nesting. Each .then() returns a new Promise, and a single .catch() handles all errors.

codeExamples.title: Async/Await Solution
codeExamples.explanation: Async/await reads like synchronous code. Use Promise.all for independent parallel operations.

notes: Async/await is syntactic sugar over Promises - it uses Promises under the hood
Use Promise.all() for parallel operations instead of sequential await
A forgotten await returns a Promise object instead of the resolved value
Error handling: try/catch for async/await, .catch() for promise chains
followUps: When would you use Promise.all vs sequential await?
What happens if you forget to await a Promise?
How does error handling differ between the three patterns?
interviewTips: Show the progression: callbacks -> promises -> async/await
Emphasize that async/await is Promises underneath
Mention Promise.all for parallel execution - it shows performance awareness

content.question: What are Symbols in JavaScript? What are well-known Symbols?
content.answer:
Symbol is a primitive data type introduced in ES6. Each Symbol value is guaranteed to be unique, making them ideal for:

1. Unique object property keys (no name collisions)
2. Defining object behavior with well-known Symbols
3. Creating "hidden" properties that don't show up in enumeration

Well-known Symbols define object behavior:
- Symbol.iterator: Makes an object iterable (for...of)
- Symbol.toPrimitive: Controls type conversion
- Symbol.hasInstance: Customizes instanceof
- Symbol.toStringTag: Controls Object.prototype.toString output

codeExamples.title: Symbol Basics
codeExamples.explanation: Each Symbol() call creates a unique value. Symbol.for() uses a global registry for shared symbols.

codeExamples.title: Custom Iterable with Symbol.iterator
codeExamples.explanation: Symbol.iterator makes objects work with for...of loops and the spread operator.

notes: Symbols are not enumerable with for...in or Object.keys()
Use Object.getOwnPropertySymbols() to access Symbol keys
Symbol.for() creates shared symbols; Symbol() always creates unique ones
Symbols cannot be implicitly converted to strings - use .toString() or .description
followUps: How do you iterate over Symbol properties of an object?
What is Symbol.toPrimitive?
When would you use Symbols in a real application?
interviewTips: Lead with uniqueness - that is the core feature
The Symbol.iterator example shows practical depth
Compare to # private fields for the "hidden property" use case

content.question: What are the Proxy and Reflect APIs in JavaScript? How do they enable meta-programming?
content.answer:
Proxy wraps an object and intercepts operations (get, set, delete, etc.) via handler functions called traps. It allows you to customize fundamental object behaviors.

Reflect provides methods that mirror the Proxy traps, making it easy to call the default behavior from within a trap without losing context.

Common uses: validation on property assignment, reactive data systems (Vue 3's reactivity), access logging, default value proxies, and deep object observation.

codeExamples.title: Validation Proxy
codeExamples.explanation: The set trap intercepts every property assignment. Reflect.set() calls the default assignment behavior.

codeExamples.title: Logging and Default Values
codeExamples.output:
Getting: name
Setting: role = admin
5000
N/A
codeExamples.explanation: Proxies can transparently add logging or default value behavior without modifying the original object.

notes: Proxy cannot be polyfilled - it requires native VM support
Available traps: get, set, has, deleteProperty, apply, construct, and more
Vue 3 uses Proxy for its reactivity system, replacing Vue 2's Object.defineProperty approach
Always use Reflect inside traps to correctly handle inheritance and edge cases
followUps: How would you implement a deep reactive object using Proxy?
What is the difference between Proxy and Object.defineProperty?
Why should you use Reflect inside Proxy traps?
interviewTips: Mention Vue 3 as a real-world use case - shows practical context
Explain that Reflect mirrors the proxy traps - Reflect.get === default get behavior
Note that Proxy is non-polyfillable - important for browser support discussion

content.question: What are WeakMap and WeakSet? How do they differ from Map and Set?
content.answer:
WeakMap and WeakSet hold weak references to their keys/values, meaning the garbage collector can collect the referenced objects even if they still appear in the WeakMap/WeakSet.

Key differences from Map/Set:
- Keys (WeakMap) or values (WeakSet) must be objects
- Not iterable - no forEach, keys(), values(), or size property
- Entries are automatically removed when the referenced object is garbage collected

Use cases:
- WeakMap: storing private data or metadata associated with DOM nodes or class instances without preventing GC
- WeakSet: tracking objects that have been processed

codeExamples.title: WeakMap for Private Data
codeExamples.explanation: WeakMap stores private state keyed by the instance. When the instance is garbage collected, the data in the WeakMap is also collected.

codeExamples.title: WeakSet for Circular Reference Detection
codeExamples.output:
{ x: 1, nested: { y: 2 } }
Circular reference detected
codeExamples.explanation: WeakSet tracks seen objects without preventing GC. Using a regular Set would hold strong references and could cause memory leaks.

notes: WeakMap/WeakSet are not iterable and have no size property
Keys in WeakMap must be objects (not primitives)
They prevent memory leaks when metadata is attached to objects that might be deleted
JSON.stringify(weakMap) returns '{}' - not serializable
followUps: When would you use WeakMap instead of a regular Map?
Can you iterate over a WeakMap?
How does WeakRef differ from WeakMap?
interviewTips: Lead with the key distinction: weak references allow GC - that is the whole point
Show the private data pattern - it is the most practical use case
Mention that WeakMap is not iterable - this often surprises interviewers

content.question: What are generator functions in JavaScript? What are their use cases?
content.answer:
Generator functions (function*) return a Generator object that implements the iterator protocol. They can pause execution at each yield expression and resume from that point.

How they work:
- Calling a generator function returns a generator object (does not execute the body)
- Each call to .next() runs the body until the next yield
- .next() returns { value, done }
- yield* delegates to another iterable

Use cases: lazy infinite sequences, custom iterables, async control flow, cooperative multitasking, and data pipeline transformations.

codeExamples.title: Basic Generator and Infinite Sequence
codeExamples.explanation: Generators produce values lazily - the infinite loop never runs out of control because execution pauses at each yield.

codeExamples.title: Two-way Communication and yield*
codeExamples.output:
What is your name?
Hello Alice! How old are you?
Alice is 30 years old.
[1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
codeExamples.explanation: Passing a value to next() sends it back as the result of the yield expression. yield* delegates to another generator or iterable.

notes: Generator functions return a generator object when called - the body does not run immediately
yield pauses, return ends the generator (done: true)
Generators implement both the Iterable and Iterator protocols
async/await is built on generators and promises under the hood in many transpilers
followUps: What is the difference between yield and yield*?
How would you implement an async generator?
How do generators relate to async/await?
interviewTips: Explain the pause/resume mechanism first - that is the core concept
Show the lazy infinite sequence example - it demonstrates practical value clearly
Mention that async generators (async function*) combine both features

content.question: Explain optional chaining (?.) and nullish coalescing (??) operators. How do they differ from && and ||?
content.answer:
Optional chaining (?.) safely accesses nested properties without throwing if an intermediate value is null or undefined - it short-circuits to undefined.

Nullish coalescing (??) returns the right-hand side only when the left-hand side is null or undefined (not when it is 0, false, or empty string).

Key differences:
- obj?.prop vs obj && obj.prop: ?. is shorter and handles all nullish cases cleanly
- a ?? b vs a || b: ?? only treats null/undefined as missing; || also treats 0, '', false as falsy

codeExamples.title: Optional Chaining Patterns
codeExamples.explanation: ?. short-circuits the chain to undefined if any value is null or undefined, preventing TypeErrors.

codeExamples.title: Nullish Coalescing vs Logical OR
codeExamples.explanation: ?? is safer than || for default values when 0, false, or '' are valid - which is common with numeric and boolean config values.

notes: ?. short-circuits the entire expression stopping at first null/undefined
??= is the nullish assignment operator: x ??= defaultVal sets x only if null/undefined
?.() calls a method only if it exists - no error if undefined
Cannot use ?. on the left side of assignment: obj?.prop = value is a SyntaxError
followUps: When would ?? cause a bug if you use || instead?
What does ??= do?
Can you use optional chaining when setting a value?
interviewTips: The ?? vs || distinction is the most common interview trap - lead with numeric/boolean examples
Mention ??= for lazy initialization patterns
Show ?. on method calls (?.()), not just property access - many candidates miss this

content.question: What is currying in JavaScript? How does it differ from partial application, and what is function composition?
content.answer:
Currying transforms a function that takes multiple arguments into a sequence of functions each taking a single argument: f(a, b, c) becomes f(a)(b)(c).

Partial application fixes some arguments of a function, returning a new function expecting the remaining ones. Unlike currying, partial application is not strictly one-argument-at-a-time.

Function composition combines functions so the output of one becomes the input of the next. compose(f, g)(x) = f(g(x)). pipe is the same but left-to-right.

codeExamples.title: Currying Implementation
codeExamples.explanation: A generic curry function checks if enough arguments have been supplied. If yes, calls the original; if not, returns a new function accumulating args.

codeExamples.title: pipe and compose
codeExamples.explanation: pipe creates readable data transformation pipelines. Each function receives the output of the previous one.

notes: Currying enables point-free style and reusable specialised functions
Libraries like Ramda and lodash/fp use currying pervasively
Partial application: bind is a built-in way to partially apply: fn.bind(null, arg1)
Function composition is a core concept of functional programming
followUps: What is the difference between currying and partial application?
How would you implement memoize using currying?
When is function composition preferable to chaining methods?
interviewTips: Start with a concrete curry example (add(5)(3)) before explaining the generic implementation
Distinguish currying (one argument at a time) from partial application
Show pipe in a real data pipeline - it makes the value obvious

content.question: What is memoization in JavaScript? How do you implement it and when should you use it?
content.answer:
Memoization is an optimization that caches the results of expensive function calls based on their inputs. If called again with the same arguments, the cached result is returned instead of recomputing.

Requirements:
- The function must be pure (same input → same output, no side effects)
- Arguments must be serializable to use as cache keys

Use cases: recursive algorithms (Fibonacci), expensive computations, React.useMemo for component rendering.

codeExamples.title: Generic Memoize and Fibonacci
codeExamples.explanation: JSON.stringify converts args to a cache key. The memoized Fibonacci avoids exponential re-computation.

codeExamples.title: WeakMap-based Memoization for Objects
codeExamples.output:
Processing user...
Alice Smith
Alice Smith
codeExamples.explanation: WeakMap memoization lets the GC collect the cache when the object is no longer referenced.

notes: JSON.stringify key strategy fails for functions, circular objects, and undefined values
Memoization is a space-time tradeoff - uses more memory to reduce computation
Only memoize pure functions - side effects would not re-run on cache hits
React.useMemo and React.memo are framework-level memoizations
followUps: What are the limitations of using JSON.stringify as a cache key?
How would you add a cache size limit (LRU cache)?
What is the difference between memoization and caching?
interviewTips: Show the Fibonacci example - it makes the performance impact concrete
Mention the pure function requirement - impure functions should never be memoized
For bonus points, mention LRU (Least Recently Used) cache for bounded memory

content.question: What are property descriptors in JavaScript? How does Object.defineProperty work?
content.answer:
Every object property has a descriptor that controls its behavior. There are two types:

Data descriptor: { value, writable, enumerable, configurable }
Accessor descriptor: { get, set, enumerable, configurable }

Flags:
- writable: can the value be changed?
- enumerable: does it appear in for...in or Object.keys()?
- configurable: can the descriptor itself be changed or the property deleted?

Properties created with assignment default to all flags true; properties created with defineProperty default to all flags false.

codeExamples.title: Data Descriptor and Non-enumerable Properties
codeExamples.output:
3.14159
['PI']
hidden
{ value: 3.14159, writable: false, enumerable: true, configurable: false }
codeExamples.explanation: writable: false prevents assignment. enumerable: false hides from Object.keys() but the property is still accessible directly.

codeExamples.title: Accessor Descriptors (Getters and Setters)
codeExamples.explanation: Accessor descriptors allow computed properties and validation. The get/set functions run transparently on access and assignment.

notes: Regular assignment creates properties with { writable: true, enumerable: true, configurable: true }
defineProperty defaults to { writable: false, enumerable: false, configurable: false }
Object.freeze() sets writable: false and configurable: false on all properties
Object.getOwnPropertyDescriptors() returns all descriptors at once
followUps: What is the difference between non-writable and non-configurable?
How does Object.freeze() relate to property descriptors?
Can you change a non-configurable property descriptor?
interviewTips: Explain defaults difference: assignment vs defineProperty - interviewers test this
Accessor descriptors are the foundation of Vue 2 reactivity system
Mention Object.freeze() as the high-level API that uses these flags internally

content.question: What are the different ways to clone objects in JavaScript? What are the limitations of each?
content.answer:
Cloning approaches:

1. Spread/Object.assign: shallow copy only - nested objects still share references
2. JSON.parse(JSON.stringify()): deep clone but loses functions, undefined, Symbol, Date objects (converted to strings), circular references (throws)
3. structuredClone() (modern): true deep clone, supports Date, Map, Set, ArrayBuffer, RegExp, circular references. Does NOT clone functions or DOM nodes.
4. Custom recursive clone: complete control but adds complexity
5. Libraries: lodash cloneDeep, immer (structural sharing)

codeExamples.title: Shallow vs Deep Copy
codeExamples.explanation: Spread only copies the top level. JSON clone creates truly independent nested objects but has limitations with special types.

codeExamples.title: structuredClone and JSON Limitations
codeExamples.explanation: structuredClone is the recommended modern approach. It handles Dates, Maps, Sets, circular references, and preserves types correctly.

notes: structuredClone was added to all major browsers and Node.js 17+ in 2022
structuredClone cannot clone functions, DOM nodes, or Error objects
The Structured Clone Algorithm is also used by postMessage and IndexedDB
For deep cloning with functions, use a custom recursive clone or lodash.cloneDeep
followUps: What does JSON.stringify do with circular references?
When would you use lodash.cloneDeep over structuredClone?
What is the Structured Clone Algorithm?
interviewTips: JSON.parse/JSON.stringify is still commonly used - know its exact limitations (Date→string, functions lost)
Mention structuredClone as the modern recommendation
Shallow vs deep copy confusion is a very common interview topic

content.question: What are common design patterns used in JavaScript? Give examples of each.
content.answer:
Key JavaScript design patterns:

Observer/PubSub: objects subscribe to events, decoupling publishers from subscribers. Foundation of event emitters and reactive programming.

Singleton: ensures only one instance of a class exists. Common for stores, loggers, database connections.

Factory: creates objects without exposing instantiation logic. Useful when the type of object varies based on conditions.

Strategy: defines interchangeable algorithms behind a common interface. Useful for sorting strategies, payment methods.

Module: encapsulates private state using closures or ES modules.

codeExamples.title: Observer/EventEmitter Pattern
codeExamples.explanation: The Observer pattern decouples event producers from consumers. once() auto-removes after first call.

codeExamples.title: Singleton and Factory Patterns
codeExamples.explanation: Singleton ensures one instance via closure. Factory returns different implementations based on a parameter.

notes: ES modules (import/export) are the modern module pattern - they replace IIFEs
Node.js EventEmitter is the built-in Observer implementation
Strategy pattern is particularly useful for avoiding large if/else chains
Singleton can make testing difficult due to shared state
followUps: What are the downsides of the Singleton pattern?
How does the Observer pattern differ from the PubSub pattern?
When would you prefer the Strategy pattern over polymorphism?
interviewTips: Know which problems each pattern solves - interviewers ask about use cases, not just names
Observer/EventEmitter is the most practically used JS pattern - implement it from scratch
ES modules replaced the Module Pattern (IIFE) - acknowledge both

content.question: What causes memory leaks in JavaScript? How do you detect and prevent them?
content.answer:
Memory leaks occur when the garbage collector cannot reclaim memory because objects are still referenced but no longer needed.

Common causes:
1. Forgotten event listeners: adding listeners without removing them
2. Closures holding references: closures capturing large objects unnecessarily
3. Detached DOM nodes: DOM elements removed from the tree but still referenced in JS
4. Global variables: accidentally creating globals accumulate over time
5. Timers and intervals: setInterval callbacks referencing objects
6. Caches without eviction: unbounded Maps/objects as caches

Detection: Chrome DevTools Memory tab (heap snapshots).

codeExamples.title: Event Listener Leak and Fix
codeExamples.explanation: The bound method reference must be stored to remove the same function that was added. Without cleanup, each mount adds another listener.

codeExamples.title: Timer Leak and Detached DOM
codeExamples.explanation: Closures in timers hold strong references to captured variables. Removing a DOM node from the document does not free memory if JS still references it.

notes: WeakMap and WeakSet avoid leaks by holding weak references
Chrome DevTools: Memory tab → Heap Snapshot to identify retained objects
React: cleanup function returned from useEffect prevents listener/timer leaks
Global variables (omitting var/let/const) attach to window and persist
followUps: How do you detect a memory leak using Chrome DevTools?
Why do closures sometimes cause memory leaks?
How does WeakMap help prevent memory leaks?
interviewTips: List the four main causes: event listeners, timers, detached DOM, and unbounded caches
Mention Chrome DevTools heap snapshots - shows you know how to debug
React's useEffect cleanup is a very common interview topic - connect memory leaks to it

title: Custom Error Types and Error Handling

content.question: What are the built-in error types in JavaScript? How do you create custom error classes?
content.answer:
Built-in error types:
- Error: base class
- TypeError: wrong type (calling non-function, accessing property on null)
- ReferenceError: accessing undeclared variable
- SyntaxError: invalid JavaScript syntax (parse time)
- RangeError: value out of allowed range
- URIError: malformed URI
- EvalError: legacy eval() errors

Custom errors: extend Error class for semantic error handling, adding custom properties, and enabling instanceof checks in catch blocks.

codeExamples.title: Custom Error Classes
codeExamples.explanation: Object.setPrototypeOf fixes the instanceof chain broken in transpiled code. Custom errors allow type-specific handling and rich error metadata.

codeExamples.title: Error Handling Strategy
codeExamples.explanation: instanceof checks enable specific error handling per type. Unexpected errors should be re-thrown rather than silently swallowed.

notes: Always set this.name = this.constructor.name for correct error name in stack traces
Object.setPrototypeOf is needed for instanceof to work correctly when transpiling to ES5
Never catch errors silently - either handle them or re-throw
Use centralized error handler in Express via error middleware
followUps: What is the difference between TypeError and ReferenceError?
Why do you need Object.setPrototypeOf when extending Error?
How would you implement a global unhandled rejection handler?
interviewTips: Know the six built-in error types and what causes each
Show the custom error class pattern - demonstrates production code awareness
Mention process.on('unhandledRejection') for global error handling in Node.js

content.question: What are WebSockets? How do they compare to HTTP polling and Server-Sent Events?
content.answer:
WebSocket is a bidirectional, persistent communication protocol over a single TCP connection. After an HTTP upgrade handshake, both client and server can push messages at any time.

Comparison:
- HTTP polling: client repeatedly fetches. Simple but wastes bandwidth.
- Long polling: client holds connection until data arrives. Better but still overhead.
- Server-Sent Events (SSE): server pushes over HTTP, client receives only. Auto-reconnects.
- WebSocket: full-duplex, lowest latency.

Use WebSocket for: chat, live dashboards, multiplayer games, collaborative editing.
Use SSE for: notifications, live feeds (server-to-client only).

codeExamples.title: WebSocket Client API
codeExamples.explanation: Always check readyState before sending. Implement reconnection logic for production - WebSockets do not auto-reconnect.

codeExamples.title: Server-Sent Events (SSE)
codeExamples.explanation: SSE is simpler and auto-reconnects but is one-directional. WebSocket enables full bidirectional communication.

notes: WebSocket URL uses ws:// or wss:// (TLS); wss is required in production
readyState: CONNECTING(0), OPEN(1), CLOSING(2), CLOSED(3)
Socket.IO wraps WebSockets with fallbacks and room abstractions
WebSockets do not automatically reconnect - implement exponential backoff
followUps: How do you handle WebSocket reconnection with exponential backoff?
What is the difference between WebSocket and HTTP/2 server push?
When would SSE be preferable to WebSocket?
interviewTips: Start with the HTTP limitation (request-response) to motivate WebSocket
Know the three options: polling, SSE, WebSocket - and when to use each
Mention Socket.IO for practical knowledge; it shows production awareness

content.question: What are advanced RegExp features in JavaScript? Explain named capture groups, lookaheads, and flags.
content.answer:
Modern JavaScript RegExp supports:

Named capture groups: (?<name>pattern) - access matched groups by name

Lookahead/Lookbehind: zero-width assertions that match position without consuming characters:
- Positive lookahead: (?=...) - match only if followed by...
- Negative lookahead: (?!...) - match only if NOT followed by...
- Positive lookbehind: (?<=...) - match only if preceded by...
- Negative lookbehind: (?<!...) - match only if NOT preceded by...

Key flags: g (global), i (case-insensitive), m (multiline), s (dotAll), u (Unicode), d (indices)

Non-greedy: *? and +? match as little as possible

codeExamples.title: Named Groups and Lookaheads
codeExamples.explanation: Named groups make regex more readable. Lookaheads assert context without including it in the match.

codeExamples.title: Flags and Non-Greedy Matching
codeExamples.explanation: Non-greedy +? stops at the first possible match. matchAll with g flag returns all matches. The s flag makes dot match newlines.

notes: Named groups: (?<name>...) added in ES2018
Lookbehind assertions: (?<=...) and (?<!...) added in ES2018
The d flag (hasIndices) was added in ES2022
String.prototype.replaceAll requires the g flag or throws a TypeError
followUps: What is the difference between /g flag and matchAll()?
How do named groups improve regex maintainability?
What does the s flag do in RegExp?
interviewTips: Show named groups with a real example (date parsing) - demonstrates professional regex usage
Greedy vs non-greedy HTML parsing example is a classic interview question
Know the 6 main flags: g, i, m, s, u, d - and what each does

content.question: What are common JavaScript performance optimization techniques? Explain debouncing and throttling.
content.answer:
Key performance techniques:

Debounce: delays execution until a pause in events. Use for search input, window resize - runs once after the user stops.

Throttle: limits execution to at most once per N ms. Use for scroll events, mousemove - ensures consistent rate.

requestAnimationFrame: schedules visual updates in sync with the browser's repaint cycle (~60fps). Avoids jank.

Web Workers: runs CPU-intensive code on a background thread, keeping the UI thread responsive.

Virtual scrolling: only renders visible rows.
Code splitting & lazy loading: load only what is needed.

codeExamples.title: Debounce and Throttle
codeExamples.explanation: Debounce groups rapid events into one. Throttle limits frequency. Both prevent excessive callback execution.

codeExamples.title: requestAnimationFrame and Web Workers
codeExamples.explanation: rAF batches DOM updates with the browser paint cycle. Web Workers move CPU work off the main thread.

notes: Debounce is for 'wait until quiet', throttle is for 'run at most N times per second'
Avoid layout thrashing: reading layout then writing in a loop causes forced reflows
Web Workers have no DOM access - communicate via postMessage
Use will-change: transform CSS for GPU-accelerated animations
followUps: What is layout thrashing and how do you avoid it?
When would you use requestAnimationFrame instead of CSS animations?
How do Web Workers communicate with the main thread?
interviewTips: Implement debounce and throttle from scratch - very common coding interview question
Explain the difference clearly: debounce = delay, throttle = rate limit
Mention rAF for animations and Web Workers for CPU tasks - shows depth

content.question: What are tagged template literals in JavaScript? What are practical use cases?
content.answer:
Tagged template literals allow a function (the tag) to process a template literal. The tag function receives the static string parts as an array and the interpolated values as separate arguments.

Syntax: tag`template ${expr} string`

Tag function signature: tag(strings, ...values)

Practical uses:
- SQL query builders: escape values to prevent SQL injection
- HTML sanitization: escape user content to prevent XSS
- i18n/translation: localize strings
- styled-components: CSS-in-JS
- GraphQL: gql tag in Apollo Client

codeExamples.title: HTML Escaping with Tagged Templates
codeExamples.explanation: The html tag automatically escapes interpolated values. This prevents XSS when building HTML strings from user input.

codeExamples.title: SQL Builder Pattern
codeExamples.output:
SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $1 AND role = $2
[42, 'admin']
codeExamples.explanation: The sql tag creates parameterized queries - values are passed as parameters, not concatenated, preventing SQL injection.

notes: strings.raw gives the raw string without processing escape sequences
styled-components and Emotion use tagged templates for CSS-in-JS
Apollo GraphQL uses gql`` to parse GraphQL query strings
strings.length === values.length + 1 always
followUps: How does String.raw use tagged templates?
How do styled-components use tagged templates with props?
What is the relationship between strings.length and values.length?
interviewTips: The security use case (XSS, SQL injection) is the most compelling real-world example
Show the tag function signature: (strings, ...values) - interviewers check if you know the API
Mention styled-components and gql - shows awareness of ecosystem usage

content.question: What is the Intersection Observer API? How does it differ from scroll event listeners?
content.answer:
The Intersection Observer API asynchronously observes when a target element enters or exits a viewport. It is far more performant than scroll event listeners because:

- It runs off the main thread (no layout thrashing)
- The browser batches observations
- No manual scroll position calculations needed

Mutation Observer observes DOM changes (child additions, attribute changes, text modifications).

Use cases:
- IntersectionObserver: lazy image loading, infinite scroll, animating elements on scroll, ad impression tracking
- MutationObserver: observing third-party DOM changes, auto-save on content edits

codeExamples.title: Lazy Loading Images
codeExamples.explanation: rootMargin pre-loads images before they enter view. observer.unobserve() stops watching once the image is loaded.

codeExamples.title: MutationObserver
codeExamples.explanation: MutationObserver receives batched MutationRecord objects. disconnect() must be called to stop observation and avoid memory leaks.

notes: IntersectionObserver is supported in all modern browsers; no IE11 support
threshold: [0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1] fires at each 25% intersection increment
MutationObserver fires at end of microtask with batched mutations
Always call disconnect() when done to prevent memory leaks
followUps: What is the performance difference between IntersectionObserver and scroll events?
What does rootMargin do in IntersectionObserver options?
How would you implement infinite scroll with IntersectionObserver?
interviewTips: The performance argument (off main thread) is the main selling point
Show lazy loading implementation - the most common real-world use case
MutationObserver shows deep DOM knowledge - a differentiator

content.question: What are async iterators and async generators in JavaScript? When would you use them?
content.answer:
An async iterator implements the async iterator protocol: each call to next() returns a Promise that resolves to { value, done }.

for await...of loops consume async iterables, awaiting each item.

Async generators (async function*) combine generators and async/await - they can use both yield and await.

Use cases:
- Streaming API responses chunk by chunk
- Paginated API calls (fetch all pages lazily)
- Processing large files line by line
- Rate-limited API calls with built-in pausing

codeExamples.title: Async Generator for Paginated API
codeExamples.explanation: Async generators pause between yields, awaiting network calls. Break early stops fetching further pages - lazy evaluation.

codeExamples.title: Streaming Response
codeExamples.explanation: ReadableStream from fetch is an async iterable. Async generators elegantly wrap streaming APIs.

notes: Symbol.asyncIterator is to async iteration what Symbol.iterator is to sync iteration
Node.js streams implement async iteration - files, HTTP requests, etc.
Array.fromAsync() (ES2024) converts an async iterable to an array
Breaking out of for await...of stops fetching further items - lazy evaluation
followUps: What is the difference between Symbol.iterator and Symbol.asyncIterator?
How do you consume a ReadableStream chunk by chunk?
What happens when you break out of a for await...of loop?
interviewTips: Paginated API fetching is the most relatable use case for interviewers
Connect to ReadableStream (fetch body) - shows practical Web API knowledge
Mention Node.js streams support async iteration - shows backend awareness

content.question: Explain Object.create(), and mixin patterns in JavaScript.
content.answer:
Object.create(proto) creates a new object with the specified prototype, enabling prototypal inheritance without constructors.

Mixins extend objects with behavior from multiple sources - a way to compose functionality without deep inheritance chains.

Object.assign(target, ...sources) shallow-copies enumerable own properties from sources into target.

OLOO (Objects Linked to Other Objects) by Kyle Simpson prefers prototypal delegation over class-based inheritance.

codeExamples.title: Object.create and Prototypal Inheritance
codeExamples.output:
Rex breathes
Rex barks
true
true
undefined
codeExamples.explanation: Object.create() sets up the prototype chain explicitly. Object.create(null) creates a pure dictionary with no inherited methods.

codeExamples.title: Mixin Factory Pattern
codeExamples.explanation: Mixin factories take a Base class and return an extended class. Composing multiple mixins adds behaviors without deep inheritance.

notes: Object.assign does shallow copy - nested objects are still shared references
Mixins solve multiple inheritance without the diamond problem
Object.create(null) is faster than {} for dictionaries and avoids prototype pollution
Prefer composition over inheritance - mixins are a flexible composition mechanism
followUps: What is the difference between Object.assign and the spread operator?
How do mixins differ from multiple inheritance?
What problem does Object.create(null) solve?
interviewTips: Object.create(null) use case - pure dictionaries without prototype pollution
Mixin factories are a practical alternative to complex inheritance hierarchies
Connect to 'prefer composition over inheritance' principle

content.question: What is the difference between ES Modules and CommonJS? When would you use each?
content.answer:
CommonJS (CJS):
- Used in Node.js historically
- require() is synchronous and dynamic
- module.exports and exports object
- Loaded at runtime

ES Modules (ESM):
- Native browser module standard and modern Node.js
- import/export syntax
- Static analysis - imports resolved at parse time
- Async loading
- Enables tree shaking

Key differences:
- ESM is static; CJS is dynamic (require() inside conditions works)
- ESM has live bindings; CJS exports values by copy
- ESM has top-level await; CJS does not
- Node.js requires .mjs or type: module in package.json for ESM

codeExamples.title: ESM vs CJS Syntax and Live Bindings
codeExamples.explanation: ESM exports are live bindings - imported values reflect changes. CJS exports are copied values. Dynamic import() enables code splitting.

codeExamples.title: Tree Shaking
codeExamples.explanation: Named exports are tree-shakeable - bundlers eliminate unused code statically. Dynamic import() enables route-level code splitting.

notes: Node.js supports ESM via .mjs files or 'type': 'module' in package.json
Top-level await is only available in ESM (not CJS)
import.meta.url gives the current module URL in ESM (replaces __filename)
Default exports are less tree-shakeable - prefer named exports in libraries
followUps: What is tree shaking and why does it require ES Modules?
What is the difference between a live binding and a value copy?
How does top-level await work in ESM?
interviewTips: Lead with the static vs dynamic distinction - it enables tree shaking
Live bindings are a common gotcha - know the difference from CJS copies
Dynamic import() for code splitting shows practical performance awareness

content.question: What are the four Promise combinator methods? When would you use each?
content.answer:
Promise.all(promises): resolves when ALL resolve, rejects immediately on any rejection. Use when you need all results and one failure should cancel all.

Promise.allSettled(promises): waits for ALL to settle. Returns array of { status, value/reason }. Use when you want all results regardless of failures.

Promise.race(promises): resolves/rejects with the first to settle. Use for timeouts or first-to-respond patterns.

Promise.any(promises): resolves with the first successful promise. Rejects only if ALL reject (AggregateError). Use when any one success is enough.

codeExamples.title: Promise.all vs Promise.allSettled
codeExamples.output:
Network error
Success: { id: 1, name: 'Alice' }
Success: [{ id: 1 }]
Failed: Network error
codeExamples.explanation: Promise.all short-circuits on first failure. Promise.allSettled always waits for all and provides status for each.

codeExamples.title: Promise.race and Promise.any
codeExamples.explanation: Promise.race is ideal for timeouts. Promise.any is ideal for redundant requests - use whichever server responds first successfully.

notes: Promise.allSettled added in ES2020; Promise.any in ES2021
Promise.any rejects with AggregateError (contains an errors array) when all reject
Promise.race can be used for any 'first to respond' scenario
Empty array: Promise.all([]) resolves to []; Promise.any([]) rejects immediately
followUps: How do you implement a timeout for a fetch request?
What is an AggregateError and when does it occur?
What does Promise.all([]) return?
interviewTips: The all vs allSettled distinction is the most common question - nail the difference
Show the timeout pattern with Promise.race - practical and elegant
Know that Promise.any rejects with AggregateError, not a regular Error
